message.content: acquanit

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

23	Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the

26	1. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
27	   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
28	   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
29	   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
30	   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
31	   leaves to match: `scope`, `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`,
32	   `account`, `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
33	   (`init.rs`, tests).
34	1. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**
35	   Distributed-slice registry — each subcommand registers itself
36	   at link time; `main.rs` discovers them via the slice rather
37	   than matching a `Commands` enum. Reduces per-subcommand
38	   touchpoints from 3 (mod decl + enum variant + match arm) to 1
39	   (registration). Costs: loses compile-time exhaustiveness
40	   (missing registration = runtime gap); help-output ordering
41	   depends on link order unless sorted; macro-magic dependency.
42	   Revisit once the `0.50.0` trait sweep's per-arm cost has been
43	   felt under real "add a subcommand" load.
44	   <https://github.com/dtolnay/linkme>

46	   shape as `linkme` — runtime-iterable registry populated by
47	   `inventory::submit!` per subcommand. Trade-offs mirror
48	   linkme's. Pick one if/when the trait sweep's match becomes

and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":

   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them, so reorder/insert without
renumbering. Reference by displayed number ("let's work on #3").
1. **single-field `options_flags` leaves → `value` field.**
   `0.47.0` introduced the convention (single-field leaf names
   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
   leaves to match: `scope`, `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`,
   `account`, `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
   (`init.rs`, tests).
1. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**
   Distributed-slice registry — each subcommand registers itself
   at link time; `main.rs` discovers them via the slice rather
   than matching a `Commands` enum. Reduces per-subcommand
   touchpoints from 3 (mod decl + enum variant + match arm) to 1
   (registration). Costs: loses compile-time exhaustiveness
   (missing registration = runtime gap); help-output ordering
   depends on link order unless sorted; macro-magic dependency.
   Revisit once the `0.50.0` trait sweep's per-arm cost has been
   felt under real "add a subcommand" load.
   <https://github.com/dtolnay/linkme>
1. **Investigate `inventory` as `linkme` alternative.** Same
   shape as `linkme` — runtime-iterable registry populated by
   `inventory::submit!` per subcommand. Trade-offs mirror
   linkme's. Pick one if/when the trait sweep's match becomes
   the felt bottleneck.
   <https://github.com/dtolnay/inventory>
1. **Drop `-R`/`--repo` from `CommonArgs` once `-s`/`--scope` is
   established.**
   - `0.49.0-2.2` kept `-R` (single path) alongside the new `-s`
     (roles, composes with `-R` as workspace root) as a
     backwards-compat alias for the migration period.
   - Once users / scripts have moved, drop `-R` — `--scope` then
     covers both path and role forms (`parse_scope` already
     handles both; only `parse_scope_roles` would go away).
1. **`-s <path>` and `-s <path>,roles` workspace-root override.**
   - Today `-s` is keyword-only (`parse_scope_roles` rejects
     paths, pointing users at `-R`).
   - Future: have `parse_scope_roles` accept a comma list mixing
     one path + role keywords, where the path is the workspace
     root.
   - `vc-x1 chid -s ../foo,bot,code` would resolve to `[../foo,
     ../foo/.claude]` (today `-R ../foo -s code,bot`).
   - Decisions to make at design time: comma-list syntax, error
     cases (multiple paths, path without roles vs
     path-as-`Scope::Single`), and whether to also accept bare
     `-s <path>` as a synonym for `-R <path>`.
1. **`por → dual` conversion.** Attach a `.claude`
   companion repo + `.vc-config.toml` to an existing por
   workspace; emit cross-links going forward. Manual
   setup on an external por workspace (2026-05-14)
   proved arduous; this should be a routine subcommand.
   Design stub in [[4]] § 2.
1. **Audit hardcoded `.claude` in diagnostics / logging.**
   `vc-x1 finalize --repo .bot` on `../dicom-rs`
   (2026-05-14) honored `--repo` throughout actual
   operations, but `bm-track` still emitted
   `.claude(main)=no-jj` — diagnostic strings have
   `.claude` baked in. Cosmetic today; load-bearing once
   bot-repo name becomes configurable (see Symmetric
   `.vc-config.toml` schema entry below).
1. **por/dual parity + `dual → por` conversion.** Make
   `por` and `dual` first-class equals (dual is primary
   today, por bolted on); add `dual → por` conversion
   (detach the `.claude` companion). Builds on the
   `--scope` rollout below. Pre-design; goal + open
   questions in the stub. [[4]]
1. **forks-multi-user + bot-data-formats follow-through.**
   Design captured across two notes; concrete work to
   land when a cycle picks it up. Major pieces:
   multi-line `ochid:` parser/emitter; bot-side
   immutability enforcement; URL-shaped ochid (per-user
   / cross-repo); vendor-subdir layout
   (`.bot/<vendor>/<version>/<id>.<ext>`) +
   flat-to-vendor migration; `.claude/` → `.bot/` rename
   (gated behind symmetric `.vc-config.toml` schema).

1	# vc-x1
2	
3	- [Overview](#vc-x1)
4	- [Architecture](ARCHITECTURE.md)
5	- [Usage](#usage)
6	  - [Revision shortcuts](#revision-shortcuts)
7	  - [Shell completion](#shell-completion)
8	  - [validate-desc](#validate-desc)
9	  - [fix-desc](#fix-desc)
10	  - [clone](#clone)
11	  - [init](#init)
12	  - [symlink](#symlink)
13	  - [finalize](#finalize)
14	  - [Testing push + finalize](#testing-push--finalize)
15	- [Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers](#cross-repo-linking-with-git-trailers)
16	- [jj Tips for Git Users](#jj-tips-for-git-users)
17	- [Thoughts for the future](#thoughts-for-the-future)
18	- [Contributing](#contributing)
19	- [License](#license)
20	
21	This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
22	We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
23	in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
24	initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
25	made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
26	and one with the conversation with the bot.
27	
28	I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
29	each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
30	of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
31	changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
32	between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
33	evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
34	most importantly **why** the code evolved.
35	
36	The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
37	(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
38	existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
39	own IDE for vc.
40	
41	See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
42	for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
43	doing so and I then created this README.md file.
44	
45	## Usage
46	
47	```
48	vc-x1 list [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # List commits in a jj repo
49	vc-x1 desc [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show full description of a commit
50	vc-x1 chid [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Print changeID(s) for a revision
51	vc-x1 show [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show commit details and diff summary
52	vc-x1 validate-desc [OPTS]                 # Validate commit descriptions
53	vc-x1 fix-desc [OPTS]                     # Fix commit descriptions (dry-run default)
54	vc-x1 clone <REPO> [NAME] [OPTS]          # Clone a dual-repo project
55	vc-x1 init <NAME> [OPTS]                  # Create a new dual-repo project
56	vc-x1 symlink [TARGET] [OPTS]             # Create Claude Code project symlink
57	vc-x1 sync [OPTS]                          # Fetch + sync both repos to their remotes
58	vc-x1 finalize --bookmark <B> [OPTS]       # Squash working copy into target
59	vc-x1 --version                            # Print version
60	vc-x1 --help                           # Print help
61	```
62	
63	**The `..` notation:** dots on the revision show which direction to
64	list commits:
65	
66	- `x..` — x at top, ancestors below (older commits)
67	- `..x` — descendants above (newer commits), x at bottom
68	- `..x..` — both directions, x in the middle
69	
70	COMMITS is the total number of commits to show (x is always included).
71	Without dots, `x` with a count defaults to `x..` (ancestors).
72	For `..x..`, the budget is split: ancestors get the extra on odd counts.
73	
74	```
75	vc-x1 list -r @              # just @ (1 commit)
76	vc-x1 list -r @.. -n 5       # @ + 4 ancestors (5 commits, @ at top)
77	vc-x1 list -r ..@ -n 3       # 2 descendants + @ (3 commits, @ at bottom)
78	vc-x1 list -r ..@.. -n 3     # 1 descendant, @, 1 ancestor (3 commits)
79	vc-x1 list -r ..@.. -n 4     # 1 descendant, @, 2 ancestors (4 commits)
80	```

  - [Shell completion](#shell-completion)
  - [validate-desc](#validate-desc)
  - [fix-desc](#fix-desc)
  - [clone](#clone)
  - [init](#init)
  - [symlink](#symlink)
  - [finalize](#finalize)
  - [Testing push + finalize](#testing-push--finalize)
- [Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers](#cross-repo-linking-with-git-trailers)

- [Thoughts for the future](#thoughts-for-the-future)

This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
and one with the conversation with the bot.

I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
most importantly **why** the code evolved.

The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
own IDE for vc.

See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
doing so and I then created this README.md file.

## Usage

```
vc-x1 list [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # List commits in a jj repo
vc-x1 desc [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show full description of a commit
vc-x1 chid [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Print changeID(s) for a revision
vc-x1 show [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show commit details and diff summary
vc-x1 validate-desc [OPTS]                 # Validate commit descriptions
vc-x1 fix-desc [OPTS]                     # Fix commit descriptions (dry-run default)
vc-x1 clone <REPO> [NAME] [OPTS]          # Clone a dual-repo project
vc-x1 init <NAME> [OPTS]                  # Create a new dual-repo project
vc-x1 symlink [TARGET] [OPTS]             # Create Claude Code project symlink
vc-x1 sync [OPTS]                          # Fetch + sync both repos to their remotes
vc-x1 finalize --bookmark <B> [OPTS]       # Squash working copy into target
vc-x1 --version                            # Print version
vc-x1 --help                           # Print help
```

**The `..` notation:** dots on the revision show which direction to
list commits:

- `x..` — x at top, ancestors below (older commits)
- `..x` — descendants above (newer commits), x at bottom
- `..x..` — both directions, x in the middle

COMMITS is the total number of commits to show (x is always included).
Without dots, `x` with a count defaults to `x..` (ancestors).
For `..x..`, the budget is split: ancestors get the extra on odd counts.

```
vc-x1 list -r @              # just @ (1 commit)
vc-x1 list -r @.. -n 5       # @ + 4 ancestors (5 commits, @ at top)
vc-x1 list -r ..@ -n 3       # 2 descendants + @ (3 commits, @ at bottom)
vc-x1 list -r ..@.. -n 3     # 1 descendant, @, 1 ancestor (3 commits)
vc-x1 list -r ..@.. -n 4     # 1 descendant, @, 2 ancestors (4 commits)
```

I've oriented myself. Quick read:

**vc-x1** — Rust CLI (v0.52.0) exploring a "vibe coding" environment built on **dual jj-git repos**: app code + a paired `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked via `ochid:` trailers in each commit so the *what* and *why* of evolution stay traceable.

**Key surface (`src/`):** subcommands `list`, `desc`, `chid`, `show`, `validate-desc`, `fix-desc`, `clone`, `init`, `symlink`, `sync`, `finalize`, `push` — all built on `clap` derive + a `Context`/`Scope` core for repo/role targeting.

**State right now:**
- No cycle in progress (`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` is empty).
- Last few commits closed out the `sb_ide` elimination (0.52.0).
- Working tree clean.

**Conventions to remember (per `CLAUDE.md`):**
- Two repos: `.` (app) and `.claude` (session); operate from project root with `-R`.
- Every commit needs an `ochid:` trailer (use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L`).
- Versioning: single-step → `X.Y.Z`; multi-step → `X.Y.Z-N` ladder ending at `X.Y.Z`.
- Use `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` for the commit→push→finalize flow with its two approval gates.
- Per-file review checkpoints; never commit/push/finalize without explicit approval.
- Doc comments on every module/function/method; `// OK:` justifications on every `unwrap*`.
- Speculation gets prefixed "The bot thinks …".

Ready for the next instruction.

content.content:
1	# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags
2	
3	Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
4	shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
5	type, parser, and tests stay together.
6	
7	This module is the CLI-layer leaf store for the options_flags
8	extraction in [`../../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../ARCHITECTURE.md);
9	see there for how the leaves fit the wider clap-args /
10	`Context`+`Params` split.
11	
12	## Architecture
13	
14	Three composition patterns:
15	
16	- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
17	  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
18	  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
19	  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
20	  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
21	  consumer.
22	- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` shared by N subcommands,
23	  picked up with one `#[command(flatten)]` line. Two forms:
24	  - *flatten-of-leaves* — composes existing leaves into a named
25	    role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle` = `dry_run` +
26	    `private` + `push_retry`). Use this when the constituent
27	    flags are themselves reused elsewhere.
28	  - *inline-fields* — holds the `#[arg]` fields directly (e.g.
29	    `common_args::CommonArgs` — the read-only commit-query arg
30	    set). Use this when a whole *set* of args is shared but the
31	    individual flags aren't reused outside the bundle —
32	    extracting per-flag leaves would buy no reuse. If one of the
33	    fields ever *is* reused (a future command wants just that
34	    flag), extract it into a leaf then and
35	    `#[command(flatten)]` it into the bundle.
36	- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
37	  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
38	  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
39	  leaf's typed value and parser.
40	
41	## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax
42	
43	Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
44	domain it carries:
45	
46	- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
47	  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
48	  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
49	  name suffix: `*Flag`.
50	- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
51	  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
52	  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
53	  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).
54	
55	Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.
56	
57	## Adding a new leaf
58	
59	1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
60	   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
61	2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
62	   (non-boolean).
63	3. Name the field(s):
64	   - **Single-field leaf** — call the field `value` (it holds the
65	     parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag
66	     explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer
67	     then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag
68	     name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`).
69	     Caveat: clap derives an arg's *id* from the field name, so
70	     if two `value`-field leaves can ever be flattened into the
71	     same struct, give each an explicit `#[arg(id = "<unique>")]`
72	     (else `clap_builder` panics — duplicate arg id `value`).
73	   - **Multi-field leaf** — descriptive per-field names
74	     (`push_retries`, `push_retry_delay`); clap derives each
75	     flag from its field name as usual.
76	   - The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`,
77	     `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`)
78	     predate this and still derive the flag from the field name;
79	     migrating them to `value` is tracked in
80	     [`../../notes/todo.md`](../../notes/todo.md).
81	4. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
82	5. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
83	   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
84	   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
85	   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
86	   handles them.
87	6. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
88	7. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.
89	
90	## Consuming an OF
91	
92	Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:
93	
94	```rust
95	#[derive(Args)]
96	pub struct MyArgs {
97	    #[command(flatten)]
98	    pub config: ConfigOption,
99	    // ...
100	}
101	```
102	
103	Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:
104	
105	```rust
106	#[derive(Args)]
107	pub struct MyArgs {
108	    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
109	    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
110	          verbatim_doc_comment)]
111	    pub config: Option<String>,
112	    // ...
113	}
114	```
115	
116	A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
117	and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
118	attributes.
119	
120	## Consumer function shape
121	
122	Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
123	relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
124	fields at the call site:
125	
126	```rust
127	// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
128	fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
129	             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }
130	
131	run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
132	```
133	
134	This wins on:
135	- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
136	  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
137	  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
138	  choice).
139	- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
140	  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
141	  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
142	- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
143	  zero-touch change at every call site.
144	
145	For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`,
146	`PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site
147	(`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the
148	pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a
149	`&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection.
150	
151	## Marker traits
152	
153	Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
154	implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
155	(their category lives in the type-name suffix).
156	
157	- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
158	  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
159	- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
160	  constituent leaf is an Option).
161	- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
162	  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.
163	
164	Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
165	a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):
166	
167	- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
168	  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
169	  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
170	- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
171	  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `ScopeParser`,
172	  `RepoParser`); bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.
173	
174	`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
175	the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
176	targets).
177	
178	All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
179	see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
180	the appropriate ones explicitly.
181	
182	## Bundle marker discipline
183	
184	Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
185	bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
186	an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
187	(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
188	drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
189	bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.
190	
191	## Pattern A worked example
192	
193	`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
194	("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
195	`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
196	surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
197	becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
198	worked example.
199	
200	## Layout note
201	
202	OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
203	README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
204	(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
205	in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
206	OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
207	to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
208	`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
209	not done preemptively.
210

# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags

Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
type, parser, and tests stay together.

This module is the CLI-layer leaf store for the options_flags
extraction in [`../../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../ARCHITECTURE.md);
see there for how the leaves fit the wider clap-args /
`Context`+`Params` split.

## Architecture

Three composition patterns:

- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
  consumer.
- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` shared by N subcommands,
  picked up with one `#[command(flatten)]` line. Two forms:
  - *flatten-of-leaves* — composes existing leaves into a named
    role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle` = `dry_run` +
    `private` + `push_retry`). Use this when the constituent
    flags are themselves reused elsewhere.
  - *inline-fields* — holds the `#[arg]` fields directly (e.g.
    `common_args::CommonArgs` — the read-only commit-query arg
    set). Use this when a whole *set* of args is shared but the
    individual flags aren't reused outside the bundle —
    extracting per-flag leaves would buy no reuse. If one of the
    fields ever *is* reused (a future command wants just that
    flag), extract it into a leaf then and
    `#[command(flatten)]` it into the bundle.
- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
  leaf's typed value and parser.

## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax

Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
domain it carries:

- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
  name suffix: `*Flag`.
- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).

Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.

## Adding a new leaf

1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
   (non-boolean).
3. Name the field(s):
   - **Single-field leaf** — call the field `value` (it holds the
     parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag
     explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer
     then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag
     name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`).
     Caveat: clap derives an arg's *id* from the field name, so
     if two `value`-field leaves can ever be flattened into the
     same struct, give each an explicit `#[arg(id = "<unique>")]`
     (else `clap_builder` panics — duplicate arg id `value`).
   - **Multi-field leaf** — descriptive per-field names
     (`push_retries`, `push_retry_delay`); clap derives each
     flag from its field name as usual.
   - The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`,
     `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`)
     predate this and still derive the flag from the field name;
     migrating them to `value` is tracked in
     [`../../notes/todo.md`](../../notes/todo.md).
4. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
5. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
   handles them.
6. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
7. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.

## Consuming an OF

Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    #[command(flatten)]
    pub config: ConfigOption,
    // ...
}
```

Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
          verbatim_doc_comment)]
    pub config: Option<String>,
    // ...
}
```

A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
attributes.

## Consumer function shape

Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
fields at the call site:

```rust
// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }

run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
```

This wins on:
- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
  choice).
- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
  zero-touch change at every call site.

For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`,
`PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site
(`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the
pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a
`&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection.

## Marker traits

Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
(their category lives in the type-name suffix).

- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is an Option).
- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.

Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):

- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `ScopeParser`,
  `RepoParser`); bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.

`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
targets).

All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
the appropriate ones explicitly.

## Bundle marker discipline

Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.

## Pattern A worked example

`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
worked example.

## Layout note

OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
not done preemptively.

content.content:
1	//! `CommonArgs` — the shared arg set for the read-only commit-query
2	//! subcommands (`chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`): a `REVISION` /
3	//! `COMMITS` positional pair, `-r`/`--revision`, `-R`/`--repo`,
4	//! `-s`/`--scope`, `-n`/`--commits`, and the `-l`/`-L` inter-repo
5	//! label pair.
6	//!
7	//! A "bundle" in the inline-fields sense (cf. `provision_bundle`'s
8	//! flatten-of-leaves form): the fields aren't extracted into per-flag
9	//! leaves because none of them is reused outside these four — if one
10	//! ever is (e.g. a future command wants just `--revision`), extract
11	//! that field into a leaf and `#[command(flatten)]` it here. Consumers
12	//! flatten this and add their own extra `#[arg]` fields (`list`'s
13	//! `-w`/`--width`, `show`'s `-f`/`--files`). See
14	//! [options_flags](README.md) for shared architecture.
15	
16	use std::path::PathBuf;
17	
18	use clap::Args;
19	
20	use crate::scope::{Scope, parse_scope_roles};
21	
22	/// Shared CLI args for the read-only commit-query subcommands —
23	/// see [Bundle](README.md#architecture).
24	///
25	/// - `pos_rev` / `pos_count` — the `REVISION` / `COMMITS` positionals
26	///   (`common::resolve_spec` reconciles them with `-r` / `-n`).
27	/// - `revision` — `-r` / `--revision` (default `@`); `..` notation is
28	///   parsed downstream by `common::parse_dot_rev`.
29	/// - `repo` / `scope` — `-R PATH` overrides the workspace root, `-s
30	///   code|bot|code,bot` selects sides; they compose
31	///   (`common::resolve_repos`). Defaults preserve today's behavior:
32	///   no flag → `[.]`, `-R foo` alone → `[foo]`. `-s` alone resolves
33	///   against `find_workspace_root()`; `-R + -s` resolves against the
34	///   `-R` path. `-s` is keyword-only today (path-via-`-s` and the
35	///   `-s <path>,roles` workspace-root override are planned — see
36	///   `notes/todo.md`).
37	/// - `limit` — `-n` / `--commits`, caps the output.
38	/// - `label` / `no_label` — `-l` / `--label` (default `===`) and
39	///   `-L` / `--no-label`; `common::resolve_header` combines them.
40	#[derive(Args, Debug)]
41	pub struct CommonArgs {
42	    /// Revision (with optional .. notation)
43	    #[arg(value_name = "REVISION")]
44	    pub pos_rev: Option<String>,
45	
46	    /// Number of commits to show (per dotted side)
47	    #[arg(value_name = "COMMITS")]
48	    pub pos_count: Option<usize>,
49	
50	    /// Revision to query
51	    #[arg(short, long, default_value = "@")]
52	    pub revision: String,
53	
54	    /// Workspace root or single jj repo path [default: .]
55	    #[arg(short = 'R', long = "repo", value_name = "PATH")]
56	    pub repo: Option<PathBuf>,
57	
58	    /// Side(s) to query; composes with --repo as workspace root
59	    #[arg(
60	        short = 's',
61	        long = "scope",
62	        value_name = "code|bot|code,bot",
63	        value_parser = parse_scope_roles
64	    )]
65	    pub scope: Option<Scope>,
66	
67	    /// Number of commits to show
68	    #[arg(short = 'n', long = "commits", value_name = "COMMITS")]
69	    pub limit: Option<usize>,
70	
71	    /// Custom label decoration between repos
72	    #[arg(
73	        short = 'l',
74	        long = "label",
75	        value_name = "TEXT",
76	        allow_hyphen_values = true,
77	        default_value = "==="
78	    )]
79	    pub label: String,
80	
81	    /// Suppress label between repos
82	    #[arg(short = 'L', long = "no-label")]
83	    pub no_label: bool,
84	}
85	
86	impl CommonArgs {
87	    /// Resolve `-R` + `-s` into the concrete repo paths to iterate
88	    /// (delegates to [`crate::common::resolve_repos`]).
89	    ///
90	    /// Bundles the `.as_deref()` / `.as_ref()` conversion ceremony
91	    /// (`Option<PathBuf>` → `Option<&Path>`,
92	    /// `Option<Scope>` → `Option<&Scope>`) into one well-named place
93	    /// so the four subcommand bodies stay clean. See
94	    /// [`../../notes/rust-idioms.md`](../../notes/rust-idioms.md)
95	    /// for why the two fields need different conversion methods
96	    /// (`PathBuf: Deref<Target = Path>`; `Scope` is a plain enum).
97	    pub fn resolve_repos(&self) -> Result<Vec<std::path::PathBuf>, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
98	        crate::common::resolve_repos(self.repo.as_deref(), self.scope.as_ref())
99	    }
100	}
101	
102	impl super::OptionFlagBundle for CommonArgs {}
103

input.description: Find all uses of scope modules

content.text:
Picture clear. Here's what I found and the open questions.

## Two `scope` modules — different things wearing the same name

**`src/scope.rs`** — *query-side*: which side(s) of an existing dual workspace to operate on.
- `Side` (Code, Bot) + `Scope::Roles(Vec<Side>)` | `Scope::Single(PathBuf)`.
- `parse_scope` handles keywords (`code`, `bot`, `code,bot`, `bot,code`) **and** path forms (`./X`, `../X`, `/X`, `~`, `~/X`).
- `parse_scope_roles` wraps it but rejects path forms (points the user at `-R/--repo`).
- Consumed by `CommonArgs` (chid/desc/list/show — `-s` field) and by `sync`, `common.rs`, `init` for runtime side dispatch.

**`src/options_flags/scope.rs`** — *creation-side*: what workspace topology to make.
- `ScopeKind::CodeBot | Por`, parser `parse_scope_kind`.
- Grammar: `code,bot` | `bot,code` | `por`. Standalone `code` or `bot` is an error.
- Consumed by `init`, `clone`, `test_helpers`.

Both surface as `--scope` / `-s` but parse different grammars. The same user typing `vc-x1 init --scope code` and `vc-x1 desc --scope code` gets two different error/success outcomes. That's the real friction — not the code duplication.

## Has options_flags hit its goal?

Partially.

- The **leaf** pattern (one `#[derive(Args)]` per flag) is working for `DryRunFlag`, `PrivateFlag`, `ConfigOption`, `ScopeOption`, `UseTemplateOption`, `RepoOption`, etc. — flatten-and-go, single source of truth.
- The **inline-fields bundle** pattern (`CommonArgs`) is doing what the README says: a fixed set of fields shared by exactly four commands, not extracted into leaves because nothing else uses them. Fine on its own terms.
- The **collision** is that the *query-side* `-R`/`-s` lives inline in `common_args.rs` and is parameterized over `Scope` from `src/scope.rs`, while the *creation-side* `-s` is the `ScopeOption` leaf parameterized over `ScopeKind`. Two parallel `--scope` surfaces, neither aware of the other. options_flags didn't fail to deduplicate them — they were never one thing — but the *name* `scope` is the same on both sides.

## On todos #4 and #5

Independent of the naming issue, the mechanical path is clean:
- `parse_scope` already builds both `Roles(...)` and `Single(path)`.
- `parse_scope_roles` is the gatekeeper that errors on paths.
- #5 extends the role parser to accept `<path>,role,role` (mixed comma list) → `Scope::Single` + role set.
- #4 then deletes the `repo: Option<PathBuf>` field from `CommonArgs` (and its `-R` flag) because `-s` covers both.

The risk is purely UX: today's `-R foo -s bot` becomes `-s foo,bot`. That's a real user-facing change; existing scripts break unless we keep `-R` as a hidden alias for a release.

## Questions before we plan

1. **The `--scope` name collision** — do you want to address it? Options:
   - Rename creation-side to `--kind` / `--topology` / `--mode`, freeing `--scope` for the query-side meaning everywhere.
   - Keep both as-is and document the duality (cheapest, but the surprise stays).
   - Unify: try to make one `Scope` cover both. The bot thinks this won't work — creation doesn't want side-granularity (`Roles(vec![Code])` makes no sense for `init`), and query doesn't want a topology choice. Different domains.
2. **Todo #4 ordering** — drop `-R` immediately, or keep it as a deprecation alias for one or two releases?
3. **Todo top entry (single-field leaves → `value`)** — straightforward sweep, but is it the priority before or after the scope work above?

content.text:
Good. Concrete proposal then, with the open questions called out.

## Unified `Scope` enum

Drop `ScopeKind` and `Scope::Single`/`Scope::Roles` in favor of one type that covers everything:

```rust
pub enum Scope {
    /// Dual workspace, one or more sides. `root` = None → discover.
    Dual { root: Option<PathBuf>, sides: Vec<Side> },

    /// Plain single repo. `root` = None → cwd / discovered dir.
    Por  { root: Option<PathBuf> },
}
```

Grammar (one parser, `parse_scope`):

| Input | Result |
|---|---|
| `code` / `bot` / `code,bot` / `bot,code` | `Dual { root: None, sides: … }` |
| `por` | `Por { root: None }` |
| `./X` / `../X` / `/X` / `~` / `~/X` | `Por { root: Some(X) }` (bare path = "single repo at X") |
| `./X,code,bot` (etc) | `Dual { root: Some(X), sides: … }` |
| `./X,por` | `Por { root: Some(X) }` (explicit form) |

Bare-path = `Por` keeps today's `Scope::Single` semantics intact ("operate on this one dir as a repo, ignore `.vc-config.toml`").

## Per-consumer validation, no second parser

`parse_scope_roles` and `parse_scope_kind` both die. Each consumer validates the rich `Scope` for what it accepts:

- **init / clone** — accept `Dual { sides: [Code, Bot] }` and `Por { … }`; reject single-side (`bot`, `code` alone). The bot thinks rejecting single-side is right because `init --scope bot` is meaningless (no code partner).
- **chid / desc / list / show / sync** — accept everything.

That replaces the keyword-vs-path gatekeeper with consumer-local validators.

## `-R` removal: error loudly

Drop the field from `CommonArgs`. Replace with a hidden `#[arg(short = 'R', long = "repo", hide = true)]` that, when present, returns an immediate error:

```
error: -R/--repo has been removed. Use -s/--scope <PATH>[,code|bot|code,bot] instead.
       Example:  -R foo -s code,bot  →  -s foo,code,bot
```

After one cycle, even the hidden alias can go. Or skip the alias entirely if you'd rather — we control all the call sites.

## Cycle shape (rough ladder, target `0.53.0`)

1. **0.53.0-0** — bump + this plan; backfill chores-11 `Commits:`.
2. **0.53.0-1** — unified `Scope` enum + new `parse_scope` in `src/scope.rs`. Old `Scope::Single`/`Scope::Roles` consumers compile against shims.
3. **0.53.0-2** — migrate `options_flags::scope.rs` to the unified type (the leaf stays; just re-parameterized). Delete `ScopeKind`/`parse_scope_kind`. Add per-consumer validators (init, clone).
4. **0.53.0-3** — extend the parser to accept the `<path>,roles` mixed form (#5).
5. **0.53.0-4** — drop `repo: Option<PathBuf>` from `CommonArgs`; wire the loud `-R` error; update call sites that compose `-R + -s` to the new mixed form.
6. **0.53.0** — close-out. Final commit, chores section, todo flips, README.md sync.

`notes/substep-test.sh` gets swept anywhere it uses `-R`.

## Open questions before bumping

1. **Bare path = Por or Dual?** I've put it as `Por`, matching today's `Single` semantics. The alternative is "bare path = dual at that root, default sides" — feels surprising; the explicit form `./X,code,bot` exists for that case. OK with the Por mapping?
2. **Should creation reject single-side?** I.e. is `init --scope code` an error or a synonym for `por`? My read: error, because keeping `code` always-means-dual-with-code-side is more consistent. (`por` is the explicit "plain" keyword.)
3. **Order: scope unification before single-field-leaf sweep (#1)?** They're independent. Doing scope first means `ScopeOption` lands in its final form before the `value`-field rename sweep, avoiding double-touch.
4. **Keep the deprecated `-R` alias for one release, or hard-remove?** Hard-remove is simpler. Either way fine; you've said UX isn't a constraint.

content.text:
Yes — and that reframing is cleaner. If path is orthogonal (always present, defaults to cwd), then *topology is also orthogonal* (it's a creation-only binary "do I want a `.claude` companion"). Once you factor both out, `-s` becomes purely about roles in an existing dual workspace, and the `--scope` collision dissolves.

## Three concerns, three flags

| Concern | Flag | Default | Who uses it |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Path** — where to look | `-R/--repo <PATH>` | cwd | query commands |
| **Roles** — which side(s) within a dual workspace | `-s/--scope code\|bot\|code,bot\|bot,code` | cwd's repo only (today's behavior) | query commands |
| **Topology** — `.claude` companion or not | `--por` boolean | dual | init, clone only |

Today's `ScopeKind { CodeBot, Por }` becomes a boolean `--por` (default false). Cleaner because the choice *is* binary — `code` and `bot` alone as topology values are already nonsense and rejected, so the apparent four-way grammar was really two-way wearing fancy clothes.

## What this changes vs my earlier proposal

- **Todo #4 (drop `-R`) and #5 (mixed `<path>,roles`) → rejected.** They were premised on folding path into `-s`, which the user has now talked us out of. `-R` stays.
- **`Scope::Single(PathBuf)` → gone.** Its job (single-repo escape hatch at query time) is handled by plain `-R <path>` with no `-s`. The runtime `Scope` enum collapses to just `Roles(Vec<Side>)` (or just `Vec<Side>` — the enum wrapper earns nothing without the second variant).
- **`parse_scope_roles` → gone.** Its job (reject path forms in the roles slot) is moot once paths aren't a roles form.
- **`options_flags::scope.rs` → gone.** `ScopeKind`/`parse_scope_kind`/`ScopeOption` all replaced by a `--por` boolean leaf in init/clone.
- **`src/scope.rs` → shrunk.** `Side` enum, the `Vec<Side>` (or `Scope`) type, `parse_scope` for `code|bot|code,bot|bot,code`, the helpers. That's it.

## On the default-`-s` musing

My read: leave it. `vc-x1 desc` with no flags acts on cwd's repo only — that's the principled "you are where you are, the tool acts here" default. Auto-promoting to dual when cwd happens to be the code side is a surprise (and is what `-s code,bot` is for, explicitly). Plenty of cases where you really do want just one side.

## Revised cycle ladder (target `0.53.0`)

1. **0.53.0-0** — bump + this plan; backfill prior chores `Commits:`.
2. **0.53.0-1** — collapse `src/scope.rs` to roles-only: drop `Single`, drop `parse_scope_roles`, simplify the parser. Update `CommonArgs`, `sync`, `common.rs`, `init` runtime uses to match.
3. **0.53.0-2** — replace `ScopeKind`/`ScopeOption` with a `PorFlag` leaf in `options_flags/`. Migrate `init`, `clone`, `test_helpers`. Delete `options_flags/scope.rs`.
4. **0.53.0-3** — sweep `notes/substep-test.sh` and README/notes for the old `--scope code,bot|por` examples.
5. **0.53.0** — close-out.

This is now smaller in scope than the version I posted last turn — three substantive steps, no `-R` removal, no path-in-`-s` work.

## Sanity checks before bumping

1. **`--por` name.** Alternatives: `--single`, `--no-bot`, `--plain`. `--por` matches the existing vocabulary in `.vc-config.toml` and CLAUDE.md, so I'd keep it; flagging for confirmation.
2. **Does `clone` always want `--por` as a *user* choice, or could it be inferred from the source URL?** If the source repo's `.vc-config.toml` already declares it dual, `clone` could detect that. But explicit beats clever; I'd keep `--por` as a manual override.
3. **Order vs todo #1 (single-field leaves → `value`).** Independent. Scope-cleanup first is still right (avoids renaming a `ScopeOption.scope` field that's about to be deleted anyway).
4. **Single-step vs multi-step?** This is bigger than mechanical — the ladder above wants multi-step (`0.53.0-N`). Confirm?

aiTitle: Review options_flags and scope argument design

content.text:
Read the current schema. Today is asymmetric:

```toml
# App side                          # Bot side
[workspace]                         [workspace]
path = "/"                          path = "/.claude"
other-repo = ".claude"              other-repo = ".."
```

`path` says "this repo's location in the workspace"; `other-repo` says "the companion's location relative to this repo." Reading the same conceptual layout takes different keys on each side. Tooling has to translate.

The symmetric form you're proposing:

```toml
# Same file on both sides:
[workspace]
code = "/"
bot  = "/.claude"
```

## Strongly in favor. Reasons:

1. **One schema, not two.** No per-side translation; both files are byte-identical. Diagnostics can name sides by their declared role without "which permutation am I reading" branching.
2. **Roles become first-class.** Today `other-repo` is implicitly the bot (when read from code) or implicitly the code (when read from bot). Symmetric schema makes the role explicit — and the *key* (`bot`) names the role while the *value* (`/.claude`) names the directory. That draws the line correctly: roles are part of the model; directory names are workspace-configurable.
3. **Unblocks the `.claude/` → `.bot/` rename** (todo #9). The on-disk name becomes a per-workspace value, not a tool-wide constant. `bm-track`'s hardcoded `.claude` diagnostic (todo #2) also goes away — replace the string with the lookup.
4. **`por ↔ dual` conversion** (todos #6, #8) becomes adding/removing one key. Today it's a per-side rewrite that has to keep the two halves in lockstep.
5. **Extensible to >2 sides.** A future vendor-subdir or docs partition adds a key. Asymmetric `other-repo` would need a list field.
6. **Ochid prefix becomes a literal lookup.** App-side trailer's `/.claude/...` is just the `bot` value verbatim; bot-side `/` is the `code` value. No "self vs other" math.

## One thing the symmetric schema needs to handle

Today's `path` field implicitly identifies *self* (the side whose root matches `path` is "this side"). With both sides sharing the same file, self-identification has to come from elsewhere. Three options:

- **(a) Infer from on-disk location** — derive cwd-relative-to-workspace-root, look up which role key has that value. Clean; no schema field needed.
- **(b) Add `self = "bot"`** — explicit, but breaks the "byte-identical on both sides" property.
- **(c) Most code doesn't need self-id** — it needs the layout map. Helpers that *do* need self-id (push trailer construction) derive it via (a).

I'd take (a)+(c). Keeps the files identical.

## Sequencing

This is a *data-layer* change; `0.53.0` was a *CLI-layer* change. Different surfaces, different risks. I'd split:

- **`0.53.0`** — `-s` roles-only, `--por` boolean, `ScopeKind` deleted. As laid out last turn.
- **`0.54.0`** — symmetric `.vc-config.toml` schema. New cycle. Includes the migration path (a small `validate-repo --migrate` or one-shot subcommand that reads the asymmetric form, infers roles, writes the symmetric form, idempotently).

Why split: the `0.53.0` runtime change is fine against either schema (it just stores `Vec<Side>`). Bundling them would inflate the ladder and tangle reviews across CLI and config layers.

Open question before bumping `0.54.0` itself: name the schema fields per **role** (`code = …`, `bot = …`) or per **kind**, leaving room for non-role-typed extensions? I'd start with role-named — extensions can add their own keys (`docs = …`) without disturbing the role keys. Flag for later.

Ready to proceed with `0.53.0` plan? Or want to keep talking schema first?

message.content: let's proceed with 0.53.0 plan

71	## chore: open sb_ide elimination (0.52.0-0)
72	
73	Commits: [[2]]
74	
75	Multi-step. The `0.50.0` cycle landed every subcommand on
76	`SubcommandRunner::dispatch` with two trait peek methods
77	(`suppress_banner` / `is_detached_exec`) and one free
78	function (`sb_ide`) carrying per-run "session chrome" —
79	the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner, `finalize::surface_previous_failures`,
80	and the `bm_track` enter/exit pair. This cycle eliminates
81	all three by relocating each behavior to where it actually
82	belongs:
83	
84	- The `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner already lands on `--help` via
85	  clap's `before_help`; printing it again at the top of
86	  every normal run is duplicate chatter the user can
87	  recover from `vc-x1 -V`. Drop the on-every-run banner.
88	- `finalize::surface_previous_failures` is a finalize-side
89	  concern; moving its trigger into `finalize` itself
90	  (rather than into a generic dispatch hook) cleans up the
91	  gate.
92	- `bm_track` enter/exit is a diagnostic; demoting it to
93	  `debug!` matches the "permanent sanity check, but quiet
94	  by default" intent already recorded in its module doc.
95	
96	After these relocations the trait's `suppress_banner` /
97	`is_detached_exec` methods have no remaining consumers,
98	and `sb_ide` has no body left to keep — all three
99	disappear. Plan-only opener; the work lands in the
100	substeps below.
101	
102	### What goes away — and the reasoning
103	
104	- **`bm_track` info-level emission.** Currently emits
105	  `bm-track enter vc-x1 X: app(main)=tracked, …` on every
106	  run. The module doc already notes "a future refinement
107	  would remove the steady-state noise without losing
108	  detection value" — `debug!` accomplishes exactly that.
109	  Detection value is preserved (run with `-v` when
110	  investigating); steady-state output gets cleaner.
111	- **`sb_ide`'s banner-on-every-run.** `clap::Command::before_help`
112	  already shows the banner on `--help`. The on-every-run
113	  emission is duplicate; users who want it explicitly have
114	  `vc-x1 -V`. Removing it shrinks the default output by one
115	  line per command.
116	- **`finalize::surface_previous_failures` gated in `sb_ide`.**
117	  This is finalize machinery — it reads
118	  `.vc-x1/finalize-fail-*` markers and surfaces them. The
119	  "don't surface from the detached child" gate is
120	  finalize's concern, not the trait's; moving the trigger
121	  into `finalize` itself lets the gate live where the
122	  marker logic lives.
123	- **`SubcommandRunner::is_detached_exec`.** With `bm_track`
124	  at `debug!` and `surface_previous_failures` relocated,
125	  no remaining consumer needs the trait's detached-exec
126	  peek. Drop the method; `finalize` reads `params.exec`
127	  directly as a finalize-private concern.
128	- **`SubcommandRunner::suppress_banner`.** With the banner
129	  gone from `sb_ide`, the `-L` flag still suppresses
130	  bookmark labels in the listing subcommands' output
131	  (chid/desc/list/show via `CommonParams::header`) — that
132	  already lives in the op layer, not the chrome layer.
133	  The trait peek has no remaining consumer; drop it,
134	  along with the `suppress_banner` field on `ChidParams`
135	  / `DescParams` / `ListParams` / `ShowParams`.
136	
137	### Ladder
138	
139	- 0.52.0-0 plan + version bump + this section + todo
140	  ladder (current)
141	- 0.52.0-1 `bm_track` → `debug!`; drop the bm_track gates
142	  in `SubcommandRunner::dispatch`

144	  - Drop banner-on-every-run from `sb_ide`
145	  - Move `finalize::surface_previous_failures` trigger
146	    into `finalize` itself
147	  - Delete `sb_ide` once both bodies have moved
148	- 0.52.0-K drop `SubcommandRunner::is_detached_exec` and
149	  `SubcommandRunner::suppress_banner` (no remaining
150	  consumers); drop `suppress_banner` fields from the

155	
156	Effectiveness is evaluated after each substep. Possible
157	outcomes at any step boundary:
158	
159	- Continue as planned.
160	- Significantly modify the shape (e.g. keep the banner
161	  somewhere else; preserve `is_detached_exec` for a use
162	  case discovered mid-cycle) — recorded in a new chores

164	- Abandon: revert the cycle to a non-eliminated

281	  removed; doc-comments updated.
282	- `src/clone.rs` / `src/finalize.rs` / `src/init/tests.rs`:
283	  test helpers updated for the `Option<Commands>`
284	  shape (`match cli.command { Commands::X(a) => … }`
285	  → `Some(Commands::X(a)) => …`).
286	- `notes/chores/chores-11.md`: backfilled
287	  `Commits: [[3]]` on the 0.52.0-1 section.
288	- `notes/todo.md`: 0.52.0-1 → `(done)`; 0.52.0-2
289	  marked `(current)`.
290	
291	## refactor: remove is_detached_exec from trait (0.52.0-3)
292	
293	Commits: [[5]]
294	
295	Third substep. The `SubcommandRunner::is_detached_exec`
296	trait method goes away, taking `sb_ide` with it. The
297	detached-exec gate is finalize-specific machinery —
298	`finalize::surface_previous_failures` is the only
299	behavior it ever protected, and `FinalizeArgs.exec` is
300	the only field that ever set it. Lifting the gate into
301	`main` (one `matches!` against `Commands::Finalize`
302	with `args.exec`) cuts the trait peek + its sole
303	override + the free function that consumed it.
304	
305	After this substep:
306	
307	- The `SubcommandRunner` trait surface is the
308	  load-bearing minimum: `to_params`, `run`, and the
309	  default `dispatch`. No more peek methods.
310	- `main` owns the surface-previous-failures call,
311	  with the exec-child skip inline at the call site.
312	  Single home for the gate; no indirection through
313	  `sb_ide` or the trait.
314	- `dispatch` shrinks to "build params, bracket with
315	  `bm_track`, run, map exit code." No session-chrome
316	  responsibility at all.
317	
318	The cycle's stated goal — eliminate `sb_ide` and both
319	trait peeks — is met after this substep. Close-out
320	lands separately as `0.52.0` (todo→done, no code).
321	
322	- `Cargo.toml`: `0.52.0-2` → `0.52.0-3`.
323	- `src/subcommand.rs`: `SubcommandRunner::is_detached_exec`
324	  removed; `dispatch` drops its `crate::sb_ide(…)`
325	  call; doc rewritten.
326	- `src/finalize.rs`: `is_detached_exec` override on
327	  `FinalizeArgs` removed.
328	- `src/main.rs`: `pub fn sb_ide` deleted (no
329	  callers); `main` adds an inline
330	  `matches!(&cmd, Commands::Finalize(args) if args.exec)`
331	  peek and calls `finalize::surface_previous_failures`
332	  before loading `Context`.
333	- `notes/chores/chores-11.md`: backfilled
334	  `Commits: [[4]]` on the 0.52.0-2 section; new
335	  0.52.0-3 section.
336	- `notes/todo.md`: 0.52.0-2 → `(done)`; 0.52.0-3
337	  marked `(current)`.
338	
339	## chore: close sb_ide elimination (0.52.0)
340	
341	Cycle close-out. The `0.50.0` Subcommand trait sweep
342	left three pieces of per-run "session chrome" hanging
343	off `SubcommandRunner` — `sb_ide` (free function) and
344	the trait peek methods `suppress_banner` /
345	`is_detached_exec`. All three are gone after this
346	cycle:
347	
348	- The on-every-run `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner is replaced by
349	  an opt-in `-V` flag that prints the banner as the
350	  first line and continues (rather than clap's
351	  exit-on-print default). Scripts can capture version
352	  *and* output in one invocation.
353	- `bm_track` enter/exit emit at `log::debug!` —
354	  default runs stay quiet, the signal is available
355	  under `-v`, and the detached `finalize --exec`
356	  child stays silent without needing a per-call gate.
357	- `finalize::surface_previous_failures` moved to a
358	  single inline call in `main` with an exec-child
359	  skip; `sb_ide` had no body left to keep and is
360	  deleted; the `is_detached_exec` trait peek (its
361	  only consumer) goes with it.
362	- `SubcommandRunner::suppress_banner` and the
363	  `suppress_banner: bool` field on the four listing
364	  `Params` types cascaded out during 0.52.0-2 when
365	  the banner emission left.
366	
367	`SubcommandRunner`'s trait surface is now the
368	load-bearing minimum: `to_params`, `run`, and the
369	default `dispatch` (which builds params, brackets
370	`bm_track` enter/exit, runs, maps exit code). No
371	peek methods, no chrome responsibility.
372	
373	### As-built ladder
374	
375	- 0.52.0-0 plan + version bump + chores-11 opener
376	  section + todo ladder
377	- 0.52.0-1 `bm_track` → `debug!`; drop bm_track
378	  gates in `dispatch`
379	- 0.52.0-2 `-V` toggles version banner (replaces
380	  clap's auto-version); banner-on-every-run gone;
381	  `suppress_banner` trait method + `Params` fields
382	  cascade out
383	- 0.52.0-3 remove `is_detached_exec` from trait;
384	  gate moves to `main` inline; `sb_ide` deleted
385	- 0.52.0 close-out
386	
387	### Outcome
388	
389	Three subtractive substeps after the opener; no
390	evaluation-gate detours. The cycle's stated goal — a
391	trait whose surface only carries what every
392	subcommand actually needs — landed cleanly. The
393	`-V`-toggles-banner shape (instead of the original
394	"drop banner entirely" sketch) emerged mid-cycle when
395	the user pointed out that opting into the version on
396	demand is more useful than removing the signal
397	altogether.
398	
399	While we were at it, the broader
400	`surface_previous_failures` design gaps (stale-forever
401	markers, concurrent surfacing double-print, mid-write
402	torn reads, no notify-at-failure path) got captured
403	as a new `## Bugs` section in `notes/todo.md`. The
404	exec-child gate covers one race; the rest is queued.
405	
406	- `Cargo.toml`: `0.52.0-3` → `0.52.0` (suffix
407	  dropped — cycle close marker).
408	- `notes/chores/chores-11.md`: backfilled
409	  `Commits: [[5]]` on the 0.52.0-3 section; new
410	  close-out section with the as-built ladder +
411	  outcome notes.
412	- `notes/todo.md`: deleted the In Progress ladder
413	  block; added `sb_ide elimination — banner off by
414	  default (-V toggles), bm_track → debug!, sb_ide +
415	  SubcommandRunner::{is_detached_exec,
416	  suppress_banner} removed (0.52.0)` to `## Done`
417	  with `[[2]]` ref.
418	
419	# References
420
